research_projects: ["Funded by:", "DST-SERB (ANRF)", "The invasion of alien species in different ecosystems, particularly savannas, is gaining wider research attention in the climate change scenario. In recent years, although many precipitation manipulations experiments have been performed in various ecosystems, the understanding of how the invasive plant-soilclimate interaction will control ecosystem processes like nutrient cycling is still lacking. This study will identify the research gap (by considering two invasive species viz., Prosopis juliflora and Leucaena leucocephala growing under rainfall conditions) and how co-occurring invasive species influence each other i.e., neutral, facilitative or competitive interaction under different precipitation regimes, how the presence of grass influence seedling growth and performance under ambient conditions and how the invasive species are mediating soil nutrient pools and processes for better adaptation under different precipitation regimes and grass competition."]

research_projects: ["Funded by:", "ANRF-IRG", "Hydrogen production technologies are rapidly evolving, but conventional methods like alkaline and PEM electrolysis are energy-intensive and limited by slow oxygen evolution reaction (OER) kinetics, high overpotential, and safety risks. The methanol oxidation reaction (MOR) offers a promising alternative, requiring significantly lower energy (0.016 V vs. 1.23 V) and enabling up to 70% energy savings while producing value-added chemicals such as formic acid. However, the lack of efficient, stable, and scalable bifunctional catalysts remains a key challenge. Current systems rely on costly precious metals, while earth-abundant alternatives face performance limitations. This work focuses on redox-state engineering and scalable synthesis methods to develop cost-effective catalysts. The approach aims to enable low-voltage, stable hydrogen production with chemical co-generation, offering a practical pathway toward decentralized and sustainable hydrogen energy systems.", "IoE_Banaras Hindu University", "Instrument Grant for establishing the “High-End Electrochemical Workstation\" at the Department of Chemistry, Institute of Science, BHU.", "SERB-SRG/2022/000138", "Research Aim: Synthesis of hollow carbon (HC) network by interconnecting HC spheres and its electrochemical applications such as Li-S battery, fuel cell, supercapacitors, and electrochemical sensors. The HC network synthesis is proposed to obtain through solution synthesis of SiO2 spheres and carbon layer coating followed by an optimized two-step calcination process. From the preliminary studies, it was learned that during the optimized calcination process, the SiO2/Carbon spheres were transformed into the SiO2/Carbon network. Careful etching of templated SiO2 spheres would result in an HC network. Such HC network structures are yet unexplored, and we are curious to apply this unique nanostructure for various electrochemical applications.", "IoE Scheme No. 6031 (B), Banaras Hindu University", "Two-dimensional (2D) materials have attracted intense interest due to their promising properties, including the direct bandgap, 2D superconductivity, and the quantum spin Hall effect, especially since the single-layer graphene, metal-organic-framework (MOF), and hexagonal boron nitride have come into the picture. Compared with the three-dimensional (3D) bulk materials, the 2D nanosheets (NSs) are more applicable due to the advantages in the uniform dispersion, ultra-smooth surface, large specific area, and massive lateral surface area. However, the partial ionic bonding between the layers and shorter interlayer distance of the 3D bulk materials makes them much more difficult to exfoliate. Solution-based exfoliation of the bulk materials such as chemical, mechanical, and hydrothermal approaches often suffer from the low-yield and aggregation. As a facile approach, it is proposed that galvanic displacement reaction (GDR)-based exfoliation of the pre-deposited transition hexacyanometalate (HCM) 2D flakes into the metal 2D NSs in the presence of chiral ligand onto the electrode surface, which could facilitate the fabrication of free-standing crystalline metal NSs at a low temperature. Most importantly, this GDR-based exfoliation method could be a universal approach for the exfoliation of 2D HCMs. Moreover, the present approach of harvesting the exfoliated, chiral ligand functionalized, and free-standing metal NSs onto the electrode surface can greatly simplify the fabrication procedure for device applications and should be of both fundamental and technological impact in emerging exfoliated metal NSs-based devices.", "IoE_Trans-Diciplinary Project, Banaras Hindu University", "Soil takes hundreds or thousands of years to form, it can be destroyed in a matter of years if not managed correctly. The management and conservation of the soil environment is arguably the biggest challenge we face as we move into the future. We need new ideas to solve the world's problems. The effect of nanomaterials on the plant system depends on the physicochemical characteristics of the nanomaterials like size, shape, surface properties, aqueous solubility, dispersibility, etc. Moreover, many Agri-nanotechnology researchers are exploring the potential of nanomaterials for protecting crops from insect pests and phytopathogenic microbes, imparting stress tolerance in plants, soil enrichment, and precision farming. Nanomaterials have high application potential in all these areas because of their unique size-dependent properties like the high surface area to volume ratio, enhanced reactivity, etc. It was observed that due to their ultrasmall size, these nanomaterials get impregnated within the cuticular layer of the insects and cause damage to this protective layer by abrasion. Some porous nanomaterials were also found to suck up lipid from the insect cuticle by capillary action. All these led to water loss from the insect body through a damaged cuticle and caused mortality because of desiccation. Similarly, a nano carrier-based delivery system ensured the slow and controlled release of agrochemicals in the field. Here, we propose to elaborate on the applications of Metal-, Metal Oxide- and Carbon nanomaterials as smart agrochemicals in sustainable agriculture."]

research_summary: Group page: https://sites.google.com/view/vkd-aospcl-bhu/overview; "God creates, we synthesize !" The theme of our group is to "REFORM, PERFORM & TRANSFORM"; Our group is centered on developing three different catalytic systems and their application in organic synthesis. The following area of research is being undertaken; (I) Merged photoredox catalysis and organocatalysis as powerful tool for molecular activation toward the synthesis of small organic molecules; (II) Catalysis by organometallic nanoparticles for building valuable C-C and C-Heteroatom bonds in organic compounds; (III) Magnetically recoverable polymer grafted graphene oxide based metal NPs as novel photocatalysts for activating CO2 toward organic synthesis
